title: RA.978 Sandwell District

translation.content: Here's a question that sounds like a trick: can a record with only 500 copies be considered era-defining? Even Brian Eno's old maxim about The Velvet Underground & Nico launching 10,000 careers at least affirmed that 10,000 people had picked up a copy—would the quip have stuck if Lou Reed & co. took a 95 percent haircut on sales? Well, scarcity can drive intrigue, and when it came time for Resident Advisor to compile our Albums of the 2010s, one with tauntingly limited availability was never in doubt: Sandwell District's noir tour de force Feed-Forward. Encompassing Karl O'Connor (Regis), Peter Sutton (Female), David Sumner (Function) and Juan Mendez (Silent Servant), Sandwell District had the underground in a vice grip for over a decade. The collective imploded in 2013 while still at apex influence, collateral damage from a Sumner-O'Connor relationship that appeared beyond repair. And yet, an unlikely second phase is now afoot, featuring retrospectives, reissues, comeback LP End Beginnings and their return to the RA Podcast after 16 years. As Sumner and O'Connor touch on in our new feature with Chloe Lula, the landscape today is vastly different to the one they first sunk their talons into. Founded in 2002 as a spiritual sister to O'Connor and Sutton's seminal Downwards label, the project truly began to hit its straps in the late '00s, as shadow-stalking cuts like Silent Servant's "Murder Murder" and the Sandwell District remix of Ben Klock's "Subzero" sliced through clubland orthodoxy like a switchblade. In parallel, the label's artwork—a recombination of William S. Burroughs cut-ups, DIY zines and arthouse amour that Mendez made his own—helped fortify a movement that placed an aesthetic premium on grit, grain and sadism. By the early 2010s, this fixation on the dark arts had utterly permeated the mood inside techno's masonic temples. Labels like Blackest Ever Black, Stroboscopic Artefacts, Avian and Modern Love were in their pomp, razor wire legends like Severed Heads and Chris & Cosey benefited from second winds, and it was briefly a jailable offence to not have a press photo in black and white. Feed-Forward was the coronating statement, with standouts like "Falling The Same Way" and "Speed + Sound (Endless)" as rousing as any in 21st-century electronic music. Sumner and O'Connor were the group's envoys on the road, a mixed blessing given they were often at each other's throats. Undeterred by lifetime bans from select German airlines for refusing to stop smoking mid-flight (really), the duo blitzed the world in a squall of snowblind largesse and sometimes reprehensible behaviour. It wasn't to last. Acolytes in the scene staggered on with downcast scowls and increasingly fetishistic art, before the whole brooding conceit started to drag and everyone resolved to cheer up a bit. After being told to bury the hatchet by no less than Mark Lanegan, Sumner and O'Connor had been performing and laying down new material with Mendez—the group's third pillar, essential for stability and balance—before his shock death last January. It's in the shadow of loss that this unique mix was forged. Featuring a stack of unheard recordings from each of the trio, as well as close allies Stefanie Parnow and Tropic of Cancer, RA.978 also gathers many of Mendez's all-time favourite songs, including Psychic TV, Grace Jones and Galaxie 500. As a way to enshrine the memory of their friend and collaborator, it's a strikingly vulnerable listen, one without many parallels on the Podcast. We're glad to run it. - Gabriel Szatan To commemorate Sandwell District's return, we decided to extend the Q&A that traditionally accompanies this Podcast into a full new feature with the duo. Enjoy: Sandwell District's Love Letter To Silent Servant
translation.blurb: Regis and Function's first mix in over a decade is a unique paean to Silent Servant, heavy on unreleased material.
